data.title: Ormat technologies outlines 2025 revenue guidance amid geothermal and energy storage growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ormat Technologies (ORA) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Doron Blachar highlighted a strong start to 2025, with a 2.5% increase in revenue and record quarterly adjusted EBITDA growth of 6.4% year-over-year. Key growth drivers included robust performance in the storage and product segments, with notable contributions from facilities like East Leamington, Montague, and Bottleneck.
  • The acquisition of the 20 MW Blue Mountain geothermal power plant for $88 million was announced, with plans to upgrade its capacity and add 13 MW of solar by 2026.
  • CFO Assi Ginzburg emphasized a revenue increase in the quarter to $229.8 million, supported by storage and product segment gains, despite a decline in the electricity segment. Gross margin was 31.7%, reflecting a decrease from the prior year due to electricity segment challenges.
  • Management introduced a restructuring in the electricity segment leadership, splitting roles to focus independently on power plant operations and drilling/exploration activities.

Outlook

  • Management projects total 2025 revenue between $935 million and $975 million, with electricity segment revenues expected at $710 million to $725 million. The product segment revenue is guided between $172 million and $187 million, while energy storage is projected at $53 million to $63 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for 2025 is forecasted in the range of $563 million to $593 million.
  • CEO Blachar reaffirmed confidence in achieving a portfolio capacity target of 2.6 GW to 2.8 GW by year-end 2028, supported by geothermal exploration and energy storage expansions.

Financial Results

  • First-quarter revenue reached $229.8 million, with $72.9 million in gross profit. Adjusted EBITDA was $150.3 million, marking a 6.4% year-over-year growth.
  • Electricity segment revenue declined by 5.8% to $180.2 million, impacted by curtailments in California and Nevada. Product segment revenue increased by 27.9% to $31.8 million, and the energy storage segment saw a near 120% revenue increase.
  • Adjusted net income attributable to shareholders was $41.5 million, or $0.68 per diluted share.
  • Total debt stood at $2.6 billion, with $690.6 million in total available liquidity.

Q&A

  • Justin Clare, ROTH Capital: Asked about the impact of tariffs on storage projects beyond 2026 and supply chain adjustments. CEO Blachar indicated multiple alternatives, including U.S.-sourced batteries and expansion into international markets such as Israel.
  • Michael Fairbanks, JPMorgan: Inquired about regulatory changes affecting geothermal growth. CEO Blachar noted expedited permitting timelines for geothermal projects on Federal land, reducing approval processes significantly.
  • Andre Adams, Oppenheimer: Queried about EBITDA contribution and investment costs for the Blue Mountain acquisition. CFO Ginzburg stated that EBITDA multiples are expected to decrease by 30%-40% post-upgrades.
  • Analysts raised concerns about curtailments in the electricity segment. Management highlighted efforts to mitigate impacts through portfolio optimization and operational enhancements.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on tariff impacts and curtailments in the electricity segment. Questions were probing yet constructive, indicating interest in the company's mitigation strategies.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing proactive measures to address tariffs and expand growth. Phrases like "we believe" and "we are confident" reinforced their positive outlook.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue rose 2.5% compared to the prior quarter, with adjusted EBITDA also showing growth.
  • Electricity segment challenges persisted, but storage and product segments demonstrated significant improvement.
  • Management's tone remained steady, with continued emphasis on addressing curtailments and tariff impacts.
  • Analysts' focus shifted slightly from operational setbacks to forward-looking strategies, reflecting increased confidence in management's plans.

Risks and Concerns

  • Curtailments in California and Nevada were highlighted as ongoing risks, potentially impacting electricity segment revenues.
  • Tariff adjustments on storage components from China could increase project costs. Management is exploring supply chain diversification to mitigate impacts.
  • Regulatory uncertainties related to the Inflation Reduction Act were noted, with management emphasizing efforts to secure safe harbor provisions for upcoming projects.
  • Maintenance work on the Puna power plant is expected to temporarily reduce electricity generation in Q2 2025, impacting revenues and EBITDA by $4 million and net profit by $3 million.

Final Takeaway

Ormat Technologies demonstrated resilience with strong performance in its storage and product segments despite challenges in the electricity segment. The company's strategic acquisitions, expanded geothermal exploration, and proactive tariff mitigation measures signal confidence in achieving long-term growth targets. Investors will likely focus on the execution of its 2025 guidance and management's ability to navigate regulatory and operational challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kimbell Royalty Partners outlines robust 2025 outlook with $0.47 Q1 distribution and significant M&A potential
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kimbell Royalty Partners (KRP) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Bob Ravnaas highlighted achievements for Q1 2025, including record revenues from oil, natural gas, and NGLs, as well as record consolidated adjusted EBITDA and cash available for distribution. He noted the completion of a $230 million acquisition in the Permian Basin and the increase in the credit facility borrowing base from $550 million to $625 million.
  • The company declared a distribution of $0.47 per common unit for Q1 2025, a 17.5% increase from Q4 2024, with approximately 70% of this distribution expected to be considered a return of capital.
  • CFO Davis Ravnaas stated that total oil, natural gas, and NGL revenues for the quarter reached $90 million, supported by 74 days of contributions from acquired production. Consolidated adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2025 was $75.5 million.
  • COO Matt Daly emphasized the robust activity on Kimbell's acreage, with 90 rigs actively drilling, representing 16% market share in the Lower 48 rigs.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed its 2025 financial and operational guidance, emphasizing confidence in achieving production goals for the year given the strong line-of-sight well inventory and resilient production profile.
  • CEO Ravnaas expressed bullishness about the U.S. oil and natural gas royalty industry and Kimbell's role as a consolidator in the sector, referencing opportunities to scale operations and expand the portfolio.
  • The company plans to maintain a steady debt leverage target of 1.5x EBITDA, enabling flexibility for future M&A activities.

Financial Results

  • Total oil, natural gas, and NGL revenues for Q1 2025 amounted to $90 million, a significant increase from Q4 2024 revenues of $69.1 million. Adjusted EBITDA also reached a record $75.5 million.
  • G&A expenses for Q1 2025 were $9.6 million, with $5.8 million of that as cash G&A expense, equating to $2.52 per BOE.
  • The company reported $299 million in outstanding debt under its revolving credit facility as of March 31, 2025, with net debt to trailing 12-month EBITDA at approximately 0.9x.
  • Approximately $251 million remained available under the secured revolving credit facility, and following debt repayments and preferred unit redemptions, net debt is expected to rise to 1.5x EBITDA.

Q&A

  • Tim Rezvan (KeyBanc Capital Markets) asked about M&A interest and how equity currency impacts acquisitions. CEO Bob Ravnaas noted a high bar for acquisitions and mentioned a potential for M&A within the next six to 18 months.
  • John Annis (Texas Capital) inquired about 2025 volumes. Ravnaas reaffirmed the guidance and stated no evidence of a near-term slowdown in activity.
  • Paul Diamond (Citibank) questioned the redemption of convertible preferred units. Ravnaas explained plans to redeem 50% of the Series A units by May 2025 and outlined a strategy to continue reducing debt while maintaining flexibility for large-scale acquisitions.
  • Noah Hungness (Bank of America) asked about strong natural gas realizations and NGL differentials. COO Daly stated that Q1 2025 figures provide a good benchmark for the remainder of the year, attributing improvements to portfolio-wide factors.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive curiosity about M&A activities and operational resilience, though concern about broader market trends and valuation challenges in natural gas basins was noted.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, particularly in their prepared remarks, emphasizing strong financial positioning and consolidation potential. CEO Ravnaas frequently reiterated the company's resilience and long-term strategy.
  • Compared to Q4 2024, the tone remained consistent, with management focusing on strategic growth and operational robustness.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue increased from $69.1 million in Q4 2024 to $90 million in Q1 2025, attributed to acquired production and higher activity levels.
  • Adjusted EBITDA grew from $59.8 million to $75.5 million quarter-over-quarter.
  • Distribution per unit rose from $0.40 in Q4 2024 to $0.47 in Q1 2025.
  • Rig activity remained stable at 90 rigs, representing a 16% market share in the Lower 48.
  • Management's confidence in guidance and production resilience remained consistent, with no significant changes in strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the potential impact of commodity price volatility on cash flows and distributions.
  • Analysts raised concerns about competition in natural gas basin M&A and valuation challenges.
  • The potential for a slowdown in drilling activity was noted, though management reported no evidence of this in Q1 2025 results.

Final Takeaway

Kimbell Royalty Partners delivered strong Q1 2025 results, marked by record revenues and distribution growth. The company remains well-positioned to capitalize on M&A opportunities while maintaining a conservative balance sheet and leveraging its diversified portfolio. Management's reaffirmed guidance and operational confidence signal a robust outlook for the remainder of 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Senate Democrats block stablecoin bill as Trump's crypto ties raise tensions
data.description:
Efforts to bring federal oversight to the stablecoin market stalled in the Senate on Thursday, with Democrats opposing advancing the bill over incomplete language and the lack of restrictions on crypto holdings by government officials.

In February, Sen. Bill Hagerty (R-TN) unveiled a bill that would create comprehensive regulatory guidelines for stablecoins, aligning with the GOP's push to foster a crypto-friendly environment under President Donald Trump.

The vote to advance the stablecoin legislation failed 48-49, falling short of the 60 votes needed to bring it up for consideration.

Support for the bill eroded among some previously favorable Democrats, who cited Trump's growing involvement in crypto as a red flag for ethical conflicts and possible corruption, CoinDesk reported.

Still, it's possible a deal could be reached in the weeks ahead, according to a Bloomberg report, with many Democrats emphasizing the need for stablecoin rules to safeguard consumers and bring greater clarity to the fast-growing space.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Geron slips as Scotiabank downgrades on sales challenges for blood cancer drug
data.description:
Shares of Geron (NASDAQ:GERN) traded lower for the third straight session on Thursday as Scotiabank downgraded the U.S. biotech, citing potential challenges to the commercial rollout of its blood cancer therapy, Rytelo.

Analyst Greg Harrison lowered his rating to Sector Perform from Sector Outperform and slashed his price target on GERN to $1.50 from $4.00 per share, noting that the downgrade was not only due to the company’s recent Q1 2025 financials, which missed the consensus for revenue.

The analyst cited concerns about Rytelo’s commercial rollout, less than a year after the FDA approval of the telomerase inhibitor as a late-line option for myelodysplastic syndromes in June 2024.

Harrison lowered his peak sales projection for the drug to $693M from $965M previously, noting that, in his view, a revamped commercial strategy “raises questions about the peak potential of Rytelo and the path to get there.”

Amid an ongoing CEO search and given the stronger risk-return profiles of rival names, Harrison doesn’t see a path for near-term outperformance that could prompt investors to buy Geron (NASDAQ:GERN) shares.

data.title: Sphere entertainment signals plans for smaller-scale Sphere expansion and new content launches in 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sphere Entertainment Co. (SPHR) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO James Dolan highlighted the company's focus on driving growth through original content, sponsorships, and operational efficiencies. He noted that Sphere's original content revenue has surpassed $500 million since its debut in October 2023, driven by strong attendance of over 0.5 million guests in the past quarter. The company plans to debut additional Sphere experiences later this year.
  • Dolan emphasized the increasing attraction of the venue for diverse music genres, including upcoming country and pop residencies, with some acts such as Dead & Company and the Eagles performing over 40 shows each, equivalent to national arena tours.
  • The CEO also cited growing interest from brands, mentioning recent partnerships with Pepsi and Google, and highlighted the venue's role in corporate events, referencing Delta Airlines' keynote event during CES.
  • CFO Robert Langer reported total company revenues of $280.6 million and adjusted operating income of $36 million for the quarter. The Sphere segment contributed $157.5 million in revenues, supported by 10 additional concerts and corporate events. Langer noted a decrease in SG&A expenses by $12.6 million year-over-year, reflecting cost optimization efforts.
  • COO Jennifer Koester discussed enhancements to the Exosphere's advertising strategy, including shorter ad spots and targeted convention-related efforts, aiming to maximize value for advertisers.

Outlook

  • Management reiterated its commitment to developing new Sphere experiences, with two productions—"Oz" and "From the Edge"—expected to leverage the venue's unique features for higher revenues.
  • Plans were revealed to design a smaller Sphere venue, which would be faster and cheaper to build, enabling expansion into both domestic and international markets. Further details are anticipated by the end of the year.
  • MSG Networks is undergoing a debt restructuring process targeted for completion by June 27, which could potentially lower operating costs and increase financial stability.

Financial Results

  • Revenues for the Sphere segment reached $157.5 million, a decline from the prior year due to reduced Exosphere advertising and Sphere experience revenues. This was partially offset by increased event-related revenues, including 10 additional concerts and corporate takeovers.
  • Adjusted operating income for the Sphere segment was $13.1 million, compared to $12.9 million in the prior year. The improvement was attributed to cost efficiencies and reduced SG&A expenses.
  • MSG Networks reported $123 million in revenues, reflecting an 11.5% decrease in subscribers and a non-carriage period by Altice. The segment's adjusted operating income was $22.8 million.
  • The company's unrestricted cash stood at $465 million, with a total debt balance of $1.34 billion.

Q&A

  • David Karnovsky, JPMorgan: Asked about international tourism trends in Las Vegas and the percentage of Sphere visitors from international markets. CEO Dolan responded that international visitors comprise over 20% of Sphere attendees and 10% for concerts, noting no significant changes in market demand.
  • Stephen Laszczyk, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about revenue expectations from new Sphere experiences. Dolan indicated that the new productions would capitalize better on the venue's medium and are expected to command higher ticket prices.
  • Peter Supino, Wolfe Research: Asked about global expansion plans and geopolitical challenges. Dolan confirmed ongoing discussions for international Spheres and revealed plans for a smaller, more cost-effective venue model.
  • David Joyce, Seaport Research Partners: Queried about Exosphere sponsorship strategies. COO Koester outlined innovations in advertising packages and formalizing agency relationships to secure recurring Exosphere ad buys.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised cautious but constructive questions, focusing on revenue drivers, content innovation, and global expansion. The tone was largely neutral to slightly positive, reflecting curiosity about growth strategies.
  • Management maintained an optimistic and confident tone during prepared remarks and Q&A sessions. CEO Dolan emphasized growth potential and operational improvements, signaling strong confidence in the company’s direction.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there was a slight shift towards more detailed discussions about operational efficiencies and new market opportunities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management's focus has shifted towards cost optimization and expanding Sphere's global footprint through smaller venues. This strategy contrasts with last quarter’s emphasis on early-stage operational learnings and initial content successes.
  • While analysts' questions in the prior quarter were more focused on the venue's initial performance metrics, the current quarter saw increased interest in long-term growth plans and new content launches.
  • Financial results showed a slight decline in revenues for the Sphere segment compared to the previous quarter due to reduced Exosphere advertising.

Risks and Concerns

  • Declining revenues from the Exosphere and Sphere experiences were highlighted, attributed to prior year Super Bowl-related advertising and lower international tourism compared to expectations.
  • MSG Networks faces risks associated with subscriber losses and debt restructuring. Management is optimistic about transitioning to a new operating model but acknowledged challenges in the broader RSN industry.
  • Expansion initiatives for smaller venues pose execution risks in terms of timing and cost management.

Final Takeaway

Sphere Entertainment Co. has demonstrated strong growth in its original content and live events, with plans to expand its footprint through innovative smaller venues and new content offerings. Strategic partnerships with major brands and cost optimization efforts are expected to support long-term profitability. However, continued focus on mitigating risks from declining Exosphere revenues and MSG Networks subscriber losses remains critical to sustaining investor confidence.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Analyst continue to stay bullish on Disney post earnings
data.description:
Disney (NYSE:DIS) on Wednesday guided strongly for the full year, posted a double beat, and added more subscribers than expected on its streaming platforms for the second quarter.

The media and entertainment conglomerate's revenue rose nearly 7% to $23.6B surpassing the average analyst estimate of $23.02B. On a per-share basis, it earned $1.45, beating the consensus estimate of $1.20. Total Disney+ subscribers were up 1.1% from Q1 at 126M, and ahead of the 123.34M estimate. 

For 2025, the company expects adjusted EPS of $5.75 and cash flow from operations of $17B. The Bloomberg consensus estimate for adj. EPS is $5.44, and cash flow is $14.8B. For Q3, it expects a modest increase in total Disney+ subscribers sequentially (estimate 2.1% growth).

Word on the Street

Needham (rating "buy" price target $125) "DIS reported revs of $23.6B (up 7% y/y, 2% above our estimate), and strong OI at $4.4B (up 15% y/y and 11% above our estimate). Each segment of OI was above our estimates. Strong profit growth was driven by DIS's Sub growth, lower production costs, as well as strength from the film studio, which included strong performance from Moana 2 and Mufasa: The Lion Kings. In addition, Experiences had stronger than estimated park attendance and spend, domestically, and international parks which benefited from a price increase."

Evercore ISI (rating "outperform" price target $134) "Disney reported strong 2Q-FY25 results and raised its FY25 adj. EPS outlook from high single-digit growth to 16%. While the portfolio clearly has cyclical risk, it doesn’t seem to have shown up in the March quarter and management’s confidence in raising guidance should dispel fears of a looming slowdown. Disney+ encouragingly posted 1.4mm net adds vs. management expecting modest net losses while ARPU growth was in line. Across revenue, upside at domestic ESPN and the domestic parks was notable (higher volumes from increase in passenger cruise days, theme park attendance, occupied room nights, and DVC unit sales while per caps also grew), though DTC missed slightly (-1.0% vs. consensus). The meaningful segment OI beat was largely driven by domestic parks and domestic linear networks followed by DTC/CSLO/consumer products. Adj. EPS grew 20% to $1.45 vs. the Street at $1.20. While FCF of $4.9bn was significantly above consensus of $1.9bn, DIS now expects a ~$2bn benefit relative to prior guidance this year on deferral of tax payments."

UBS (rating "buy" price target $120) "Disney reported solid F2Q results, led by stronger Experiences, improvement at DTC and resilient linear profits. Total revenues grew 7% y/y vs. 5% in F1Q while segment OI increased 15% vs. 31% last quarter. With Adj EPS up 32% YTD, management now expects 16% EPS growth to $5.75 in F25 (vs. HSD growth prior) with double digit growth through F27. While the macro backdrop is dynamic, mgmt suggested minimal impact to date with 2H Parks bookings solidly ahead of a year ago and sports ad remaining strong (mgmt now expects >3% total ad growth in F25 vs. prior 3% and flat in F24). We now look for $5.80 of EPS in F25E, followed by 13% growth in F26E to $6.53 (vs. $5.51/$6.06 prior). This will be led by ramping DTC profits, a return to growth in the sports segment and resilient parks trends, incl. the benefits from new cruise ships and a more flexible/variable Parks cost structure in the event of a macro downturn."

Bank of America (rating "buy" price target $140) "DIS reported a solid F2Q with revenue and operating income above our expectations. By segment, Entertainment OI was $1.26bn (vs. our $1.02bn est.), Sports OI was $687mn (vs. our $700mn est.) and Experiences OI was $2.49bn (vs. our $2.41bn est.). Free Cash Flow of $4.9bn was well above our $2.3bn estimate. DIS raised its FY25 outlook to 16% adjusted EPS growth (to $5.75) from high single digit adjusted EPS growth previously which is encouraging considering recent macro volatility. For FY3Q management now expects a modest increase in DIS+ subscribers compared to 2Q."

Seeking Alpha analyst Brett Ashcroft Green (rating "buy") "The Walt Disney Company impressed with Disney+ subscriber growth, resilient theme park demand, and a promising new international expansion model launching in Abu Dhabi. DIS shares remain historically cheap based on price-to-sales and price-to-book ratios, offering long-term value, especially considering Disney’s irreplaceable real estate assets. A live-action Lilo & Stitch film and major theme park investments in Florida and California are expected to boost engagement and revenues."

data.title: Yields jump as traders move into equities after Trump says 'go out and buy stock now'
data.description:
U.S. Treasury yields rose on Thursday, as market participants dumped bonds and piled into equities following comments from President Donald Trump on trade and the markets.

Trump announced a new trade deal with the United Kingdom - the first inking of an agreement with a trading partner since his imposition of sweeping reciprocal tariffs. He also urged traders to "go out and buy stock now" as he sees major gains for the U.S. from trade deals and his tax bill.

The benchmark U.S. 10 Year Treasury yield (US10Y) was last up 10 basis points at 4.37%. The shorter-end more rate-sensitive U.S. 2 Year Treasury yield (US2Y) had jumped 10 basis points to 3.88%, marking its highest level in nearly a month. 

Listed below are the movements of other yields across the curve. 

  • U.S. 5 Year Treasury yield (US5Y) +12 basis points to 3.98%. 
  • U.S. 20 Year Treasury yield (US20Y) +7 basis points to 4.85%. 
  • U.S. 30 Year Treasury yield (US30Y) +6 basis points to 4.83%. 

Longer-term yields in particular had slipped on Wednesday, as investors had snapped up bonds following a potential stagflation warning from the Federal Reserve. 

Market watchers can view real-time movements in Treasury yields across maturities as well on Seeking Alpha’s bond page. 

Treasury and Fixed Income ETFs: (NASDAQ:TLT), (NYSEARCA:TLH), (NASDAQ:IEF), (NASDAQ:IEI), (NASDAQ:SHY), (NYSEARCA:SGOV), (NYSEARCA:SCHO), (NYSEARCA:BIL), (NYSEARCA:AGG), (NASDAQ:BND), (NASDAQ:VCIT), (NYSEARCA:MUB), (NASDAQ:MBB), (NYSEARCA:JNK), (NYSEARCA:LQD), (NYSEARCA:HYG), and (NYSEARCA:TIP).
